title: podman-run terminal output is written to syslog and journald among others
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

If you run any container with podman with terminal output that output is written to `/var/log/syslog`, `/var/log/messages`, `/var/log/user.log` and journald on Debian (on Fedora it's only journald as far as I can tell). 

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. `podman run --rm alpine echo "A message to my syslog"`

**Describe the results you received:**
The message pops up in `/var/log/syslog`, `/var/log/messages`, `/var/log/user.log` and journald on Debian (on Fedora journald).

**Describe the results you expected:**
I would expect to either have no output at all from what happens inside the container or only errors. 

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

The problem is exaggerated if you want to redirect output from the container i.e. `mysqldump` you quickly fill up your whole drive.

`--log-driver=none` is a workaround. `--log-opt max-size=<size>` does nothing (expected).

**Output of `podman version`:**
Debian:
```
Version:      3.4.7
API Version:  3.4.7
Go Version:   go1.18.1
Built:        Thu Jan  1 01:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

Fedora:
```
Client:       Podman Engine
Version:      4.1.1
API Version:  4.1.1
Go Version:   go1.18.3
Built:        Wed Jun 22 18:17:44 2022
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide? (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md)**

Yes, with latest version 4.1.1 (on Fedora) and couldn't find anything related in the Troubleshooting Guide.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body:
This is the expected behaviour. The default log driver is journald and Podman will only log to journald. It is up to journald to rotate the journal and keep a reasonable size of messages. 
If debian then stores the messages three times I would argue that this a debian problem and not Podman.

You can also use `--log-driver=k8s-file` if you still want a log file but not in the journal, this should respect `--log-opt max-size=<size>`

alse see https://github.com/containers/podman/discussions/14743

Thanks for the response, however it's weird. The default behavior is practically broken on probably every Debian-based distribution. And even on Fedora you are not able to read your journal anymore once you pipe some amount of data. And it is also writing this data at least twice (five times in the case of Debian). 

This is certainly a feature for debugging, but pretty wasteful as the default I think.

> And it is also writing this data at least twice

Who is writing twice? The conmon process will capture the container stdout/err and writes that to the journal. If anything is written more than once it is not done by us.

> Who is writing twice? The conmon process will capture the container stdout/err and writes that to the journal. If anything is written more than once it is not done by us.

The redirection so to speak. `podman run --rm alpine command > file` is writing it once to file and (at least) once to log.

That is a fair point but Podman cannot control this. The main reason why we have to log somewhere is because `podman logs` cannot work without it. If you do not plan on using `podman logs` then use `--log-driver=none` you can also set the default in containers.conf.

Podman cannot alter its behavior on redirection, that makes sense. It also makes sense that you want the option for verbose logs. What does not make as much sense to me is that these verbose logs are the default.

At this point people might expect this behavior tho, so even if you would agree, it's maybe not changeable anyway if this is a behavior heavily relied upon.

I mean at least it's fixable.

Yes the standard was made by Docker many years ago to allow users to go back to docker logs to see the STDOUT and STDERR from a container.  In Podman the default can be changed as @Luap99 stated by setting default logging to NONE.
